text: Hello everyone. This is a video of a routine robotic right indirect inguinal hernia repair with three D max mid XL.
I'm Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California at ultimate summit medical center with the first surgical consultant group.
The surgeon begins the procedure by making a vertical oblique incision on the lateral side, initiating the surgical approach for the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon creates a flap starting just medial to the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), following the incision across the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies an island of fat as a landmark to begin the procedure, using it as a visual guide for the surgical approach.
The surgeon aims to achieve approximately four centimeters of mesh overlap lateral to the internal ring during the robotic right indirect inguinal hernia repair.
Here I'm trying to stay outside of the retroactive space, but that's not absolutely necessary. I kind of go with the flow, whatever is easiest.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, noting that medially, the retroactive space is more accessible, while laterally, they remain outside of it.
When we do a tapping on a hernia repair, we're in the retroactive space, so there's definitely nothing wrong with being in the retroactive space.
The surgeon navigates laterally in the space of Bogros, aiming to stay close to the peritoneum while preserving the preperitoneal fat layer.
Good retraction with the left hand is definitely very important.
By the way, this is a normal speed, not sped up.
I always tell folks to create cotton candy with the left hand. So if your left hand is retracting well, you'll get lots of aerial or tissue that will help with your dissection and keep it hemostatic.

And hemostasis is extremely important. Because at the start of the case, it's not going to affect your dissection. But as you move along, if there's bleeding, blood will pull posteriorly and make the most critical part of the dissection difficult.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to reach the peritoneum, using gentle pushing to enter the correct anatomical plane, while maintaining proximity to the preperitoneal fat.
The surgeon uses gentle pushing to enter the correct anatomical plane, while cautery is occasionally applied to assist in navigating the posterior region.
I always like to work broadly before going away or too deep.
The surgeon cuts the transverse salus sling, which is positioned around the vas deferens, although the vas deferens itself is not currently visible.
The surgical field reveals very thin patient tissues with minimal subcutaneous fat, highlighting the need for precision due to the limited margin for error.
The surgeon uses cautery to enter the correct plane, separating the testicular vessels from the hernia sac.
Under ten times magnification, the robotic instruments are used to carefully dissect the small vessels from the cord structures, showcasing precise manipulation of delicate tissues.

The surgeon maintains a bloodless dissection, revealing clear tissue planes. After removing the testicular vessels, the surgeon scores around the hernia sac to detach surrounding fatty tissue and small vessels.
The surgeon uses cautery to carefully dissect around the hernia sac, gradually revealing the tip of the hernia sac amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses precision dissection to remove small vessels from the surface of the hernia sac, visible under magnification.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, identifying and dividing non-white tissue structures to separate them from the sac.
The surgeon carefully divides the attachments of the testicular vessels to the hernia sac, facilitating the gradual emergence of the sac from the internal ring. The tip of the sac is visible.
The surgeon works broadly around the hernia sac, occasionally creating small holes in the sac, which are easily repaired. As the sac is manipulated, areas further away become easier to manage.
The surgeon uses the left hand to retract aerial tissue, maintaining a clear surgical field without any visible bleeding.

The surgeon positions the mesh to cross the midline, ensuring it extends two centimeters deep into the pelvis. The mesh is carefully tucked behind a vessel referred to as 'Nolan,' named after a fellow.
The surgeon identifies the pubis and proceeds to cross the midline during the inguinal hernia repair, ensuring the mesh placement adheres to established surgical guidelines.
The surgeon pauses momentarily before continuing the dissection laterally, aiming to separate all visible vessels from the peritoneum in a lateral direction.

The surgeon parietalizes the cord by peeling the peritoneum off the testicular vessels and the vas deferens, ensuring proper placement of the mesh. The external iliac artery is visible in the surgical field.
The robotic instruments maneuver with precision near critical anatomical structures, avoiding any injury while maintaining close proximity during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects medial to the external iliac vein, revealing an aberrant vessel branching from the external iliac and connecting with the obturator vessels.

The surgeon exposes the fascial plane over the iliac vessels, including the iliac artery and vein, as well as laterally over the genital branch, genitofemoral nerve, femoral branch, and lateral cutaneous nerve.
The surgeon demonstrates the preservation of a fascial layer over the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, ensuring the mesh does not directly contact critical structures, even in a thin patient.
The surgeon replaces the scissors with a needle driver, having already pre-placed the mesh and two sutures into the abdomen prior to docking the robot.
The surgeon stands the mesh upright and pushes it past the midline, ensuring it remains open and properly positioned without folding.
The surgeon assesses the lateral dissection area, noting that the pocket created is small and requires adjustments. The dissection continues in the correct plane to expand the space.
The surgeon tucks the mesh into the retropubic space, aiming to align the vertical blue line over the vas deferens for proper placement.
The surgeon uses a grasper and needle driver to bluntly dissect and expand the pocket for mesh placement, ensuring no peritoneum is trapped behind the mesh.
The surgeon identifies peritoneum sitting behind the mesh and performs additional dissection to carefully remove the peritoneum, ensuring it does not remain tucked behind the mesh.
The surgeon performs additional dissection using two graspers and a needle driver to tuck the mesh into the correct anatomical plane, ensuring it is properly positioned without folding.
The mesh is positioned over the myopectineal orifice with some flexibility to adjust during desufflation. A stitch is placed to secure the mesh to Cooper's ligament, ensuring stable coverage.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to secure the mesh in place, preventing it from lifting as the bladder rises. The retropubic space has been deeply dissected to ensure the mesh remains stable.

The surgeon places a suture superior laterally to prevent the mesh from rotating counterclockwise on the right side and to stop the peritoneum from sneaking behind the mesh posterior laterally. The stitch is positioned to avoid the triangle of pain and triangle of doom.
The surgeon prepares to take a picture of the surgical field before closing the peritoneal flap, ensuring the mesh is properly positioned and the peritoneum is not tucked behind it.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock suture to close the peritoneal flap, starting laterally. The needle is kept in the right hand to maintain efficiency during suture placement. The surgeon carefully aligns the edges of the lower flap to avoid excessive tension on the peritoneum, ensuring the flap comes together smoothly without being too taut.

I feel like I'm never able to see a And I were able to center everything and see everything exactly the way I want to see it. Zero certainly is much better.
The surgeon closes a small hole in the hernia sac using a figure-of-eight suture technique with a 3-0 vicryl suture.
The surgeon inspects the lower edge of the mesh, ensuring that no peritoneum is sneaking in behind it to prevent posterior lateral recurrence.
